walkthrough.title: Unfold the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Concept ready to play! First, unfold the big game board and place it in the center of the table. This board is covered with universal icons -- pictures representing all kinds of ideas like colors, sizes, animals, jobs, places, and more. Make sure everyone can see and reach the board easily. This board is the heart of the game -- it's how you'll communicate without words.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Concept Cards
walkthrough.text: Now grab the deck of 110 Concept cards. Each card has 9 words or phrases organized into three difficulty levels: Easy (marked in green), Hard (marked in orange), and Challenging (marked in red). Give the deck a good shuffle and place it face down near the board where everyone can reach it.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Victory Point Tokens
walkthrough.text: Find the victory point tokens. There are two types: Double tokens worth 2 VPs each, and Single tokens worth 1 VP each. Create a pile of exactly 12 Double VP tokens -- these determine when the game ends. Then make a generous pile of Single VP tokens nearby. Keep both piles within easy reach of all players.

walkthrough.title: Meet the Main Concept Pawn
walkthrough.text: Now let's talk about the star of the show -- the green pawn set. The green set has one large question mark pawn (that's the Main Concept pawn) and 10 green cubes. The Main Concept pawn is always placed first on the board to represent the big-picture category of whatever word you're trying to get people to guess. The green cubes then add detail to that main idea.

walkthrough.title: Meet the Sub-Concept Pawns
walkthrough.text: In addition to the green set, there are four more sets in blue, red, yellow, and black. Each set has one exclamation mark pawn (the Sub-Concept pawn) and 8 cubes of matching color. Sub-concepts let you layer additional clues on top of the main concept. For example, if your main concept is 'a building,' a sub-concept might indicate the country it's in. Place all pawns and cubes in the storage bowl where everyone can grab them.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Aids
walkthrough.text: The game comes with 2 player aids that list possible meanings for the universal icons on the board. Pass these around or set them where people can glance at them. The icon meanings are not exhaustive -- they're just suggestions to spark ideas. As you play more, you'll discover creative uses the player aids don't even mention!

walkthrough.title: Form the First Team
walkthrough.text: In Concept, a team of two neighbors at the table work together to give clues. Pick any method to determine the first team -- draw cards, youngest players, volunteers, whatever feels right. The two teammates must be sitting next to each other at the table. They'll work together placing pawns and cubes to get everyone else to guess their word.

walkthrough.title: Quick Overview Before You Start
walkthrough.text: Here's the big picture: the team draws a card, picks a word, and places pieces on the board icons to get others to guess. The first player to guess correctly gets a 2 VP Double token. Each team member gets a 1 VP Single token. Then the next two clockwise neighbors become the new team. The game ends when all 12 Double tokens are claimed. Simple as that -- let's learn the details!

bullets: Place the icon board in the center of the table

The 12 Double tokens determine game length

summary.title: Form the First Team
bullets: Team = 2 players sitting next to each other
Choose first team by any method you like
Team gives clues; everyone else guesses

summary.title: Quick Overview Before You Start
bullets: Team draws a card, picks a word, places pieces on icons
First correct guesser: 2 VP Double token
Each team member: 1 VP Single token
Game ends when all 12 Double tokens are claimed

walkthrough.title: Drawing a Card and Choosing a Word
walkthrough.text: The team draws the top Concept card and looks at the 9 words listed. Together, they secretly pick one word or phrase for the other players to guess. For your first few games, stick to the Easy words (marked in green). Hard words require more creative clue-giving, and Challenging words require that everyone at the table really understands how the icons work. Don't show the card to anyone else!

walkthrough.title: Placing the Main Concept Pawn
walkthrough.text: The team always starts by placing the green Main Concept pawn (the big question mark) on an icon that represents the broadest category of the word. For example, if the answer is 'milk,' you might place the green pawn on the food/drink icon. If the answer is 'fish,' place it on the animal icon. This tells everyone: 'We're looking for something in this category.' Think of it as the headline of your clue.

walkthrough.title: Adding Green Cubes for Detail
walkthrough.text: After placing the green pawn, add green cubes to other icons to narrow down the main concept. Sticking with the milk example: place a green cube on 'white' and another on 'liquid.' Now everyone knows it's a white liquid you eat or drink. You have 10 green cubes, so you can add as many details as needed. The order in which you place cubes can matter -- it can emphasize which clues are most important.

walkthrough.title: Using Sub-Concepts
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the main concept alone isn't enough. That's where sub-concepts come in! Grab a differently colored pawn (blue, red, yellow, or black exclamation mark) and place it on an icon to start a new thread of clues. Then add cubes of that same color to build out the sub-concept. For example, if you're cluing 'Eiffel Tower,' the green pawn might go on 'building' with green cubes on 'metallic.' Then a blue sub-concept pawn on 'place/country' with blue cubes on the French flag colors. Sub-concepts clarify the main idea.

walkthrough.title: Guessing Rules
walkthrough.text: While the team places pieces, everyone else shouts out guesses freely -- there's no turn order for guessing, and you can guess as many times as you want! There's no penalty for wrong guesses, so fire away. The team can say 'yes' or nod when someone is on the right track, but they cannot speak any other words, point at objects in the room, make gestures, or give any other verbal hints. The only communication is through the pawns and cubes on the board.

walkthrough.title: Scoring a Correct Guess
walkthrough.text: When someone guesses the exact word or phrase, celebration time! The first player to guess correctly takes 1 Double VP token (worth 2 points) from the pile. Each member of the clue-giving team takes 1 Single VP token (worth 1 point each). So the guesser gets 2 VPs and each team member gets 1 VP. Then clear all pawns and cubes off the board back into the bowl.

walkthrough.title: Rotating to the Next Team
walkthrough.text: After a word is guessed (or abandoned), the next two players clockwise around the table become the new clue-giving team. They draw a new card, pick a word, and start placing pieces. The game keeps rotating like this, so everyone gets multiple chances to be on a team.

walkthrough.title: When Nobody Can Guess
walkthrough.text: If guessing stalls, the team has options. They can remove all their pieces and start over with a completely different approach to the same word. They can also invite a third player to join the team and help give clues. But if even with help nobody can crack it, the team abandons the word -- no one scores any points that round. Don't feel bad! Some words are just tricky. Move on to the next team.

walkthrough.title: Important Clarifications
walkthrough.text: A few key details to keep in mind. Team members should quietly consult with each other about their clue strategy -- they might not be on the same wavelength! Either team member can place pieces at any time; they don't need to take turns. The order cubes are placed can be important, so don't hesitate to remove and replace cubes to change the emphasis. There's no limit to how many pawns and cubes you use, but be careful not to overwhelm the guessers with too much information at once.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Winning
walkthrough.text: The game ends as soon as the last of the 12 Double VP tokens is claimed. Everyone counts up their victory points from both Double and Single tokens. The player with the most VPs wins! If there's a tie, share the victory -- this is a party game after all. And here's a fun secret from the game designers: during their testing, they eventually dropped the scoring entirely and just played for the joy of guessing. Feel free to do the same if your group prefers a more relaxed experience!

walkthrough.title: Optional Difficulty Variants
walkthrough.text: The rulebook mentions that no extra points are awarded for Hard or Challenging words by default. But if your group wants more incentive, you can house-rule bonus VPs for harder words -- for example, 1 or 2 extra VP for the team and the guesser when a Hard or Challenging word is successfully guessed. This encourages braver word choices and keeps experienced players engaged.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Game
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through a practice round of Concept with four players -- Alice, Bob, Carol, and Dave. Alice and Bob are sitting next to each other, so they'll be the first clue-giving team. Carol and Dave will guess. Follow along even if you have a different number of players. Ready? Let's go!

walkthrough.title: The Team Draws a Card
walkthrough.text: Alice draws the top card. She and Bob look at it together, keeping it hidden from Carol and Dave. The Easy words on the card include 'milk,' 'guitar,' and 'dinosaur.' Since this is their first game, they pick 'milk' -- a straightforward word that should be fun to clue with icons.

walkthrough.title: Alice Places the Main Concept Pawn
walkthrough.text: Alice grabs the green question mark pawn and places it on the food/drink icon on the board. This tells Carol and Dave: 'We're looking for something related to food or drink.' Good start -- but that covers a LOT of possibilities. Time to narrow it down.

walkthrough.title: Bob Adds Green Cubes
walkthrough.text: Bob picks up a green cube and places it on the 'white' color icon. Then he adds another green cube on the 'liquid' icon. Now Carol and Dave know: it's a white liquid that's food or drink. Carol shouts 'Milk!' and she's right! That was fast!

walkthrough.title: Scoring the Round
walkthrough.text: Carol guessed correctly, so she takes a Double VP token (worth 2 points). Alice and Bob each take a Single VP token (worth 1 point each). All green pieces come off the board and go back in the bowl. That's one round done!

walkthrough.title: New Team: Carol and Dave
walkthrough.text: Now the team rotates clockwise. Carol and Dave are the new clue-giving team. They draw a card and see an interesting word among the Easy options: 'dinosaur.' Carol's eyes light up. Dave nods. Let's see how they handle this one.

walkthrough.title: Starting with the Main Concept
walkthrough.text: Carol places the green question mark pawn on the 'animal' icon. Good call -- dinosaur is an animal. Dave adds a green cube on 'large/big' and another on the icon representing 'old' or 'long ago/past.' Alice shouts 'Elephant!' -- big and old, right? But no! Carol shakes her head. They need more clues.

walkthrough.title: Adding the Key Detail
walkthrough.text: Dave places another green cube on the 'green' color icon. A large, old, green animal? Bob shouts 'Dinosaur!' and that's it! Carol and Dave high-five. Bob collects a Double VP token, and Carol and Dave each get a Single VP token. Pieces go back in the bowl.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake #1: Too Many Clues at Once
walkthrough.text: Now it's Dave and Alice's turn as the new team. They draw a card and pick 'baseball.' Dave gets excited and immediately places the green pawn on 'sport,' then drops green cubes on 'ball,' 'white,' 'cylindrical,' 'wood,' and 'USA' all at once. Bob and Carol stare at the board, overwhelmed. 'Cricket?' 'Bowling?' 'Hockey?' Nobody's getting it because there's too much information with no structure.

walkthrough.title: How to Fix It: Build Up Gradually
walkthrough.text: Alice whispers to Dave, 'Let's start over.' They clear the board. This time, Alice places the green pawn on 'sport' and one green cube on 'ball' -- then pauses. A sport with a ball. Carol guesses 'Soccer?' 'Tennis?' Not yet. Dave adds a green cube on 'white.' Bob says 'Golf?' Getting warmer. Now they add a sub-concept.

walkthrough.title: Using Sub-Concepts Effectively
walkthrough.text: Alice grabs the blue exclamation mark pawn and places it on the 'tool/object' icon. She adds a blue cube on 'cylindrical' and another on 'wood.' So the sub-concept is a cylindrical wooden tool associated with this sport. Bob shouts 'Baseball!' and he's right -- the bat was the key! The lesson: build your clues in layers, not all at once.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake #2: Forgetting Sub-Concepts Exist
walkthrough.text: Next team: Bob and Carol. They pick 'Eiffel Tower.' Bob places the green pawn on 'building' and adds green cubes on 'metallic,' 'tall,' and 'famous.' Alice guesses 'Empire State Building?' 'Statue of Liberty?' 'Big Ben?' The clues are too generic. Bob tries adding 'old' and 'tourism' -- still too vague. The problem? They're trying to do everything with just the green set.

walkthrough.title: The Fix: Layer with Sub-Concepts
walkthrough.text: Carol jumps in and grabs the red exclamation mark pawn. She places it on 'place/country' and adds red cubes on 'blue,' 'white,' and 'red' -- the colors of the French flag! Dave immediately shouts 'Eiffel Tower!' The sub-concept about France unlocked the answer instantly. Remember: the main concept describes what it IS, and sub-concepts describe the context around it.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake #3: Talking or Gesturing
walkthrough.text: Alice and Dave are up again. They pick 'cowboy.' Alice places the green pawn on 'man/male' and a green cube on 'job/profession.' Dave is so excited that he blurts out 'Think about the Wild West!' -- oops! That's a rule violation. The team can only say 'yes' to confirm correct guesses or nod encouragingly. No words, no pointing at things in the room, no acting things out. This isn't charades!

walkthrough.title: Getting Back on Track
walkthrough.text: Alice gives Dave a look, and he zips his lips. She adds a green cube on 'fight/conflict.' Then she grabs the blue pawn for a sub-concept, placing it on 'animal' with blue cubes on 'large,' 'brown,' and 'fast.' A big, brown, fast animal. Carol guesses 'Horse?' Alice nods enthusiastically -- they're on the right track!

walkthrough.title: Layering the Final Clue
walkthrough.text: Dave adds a green cube on the 'above/on top of' icon, connecting the person to the animal. A man whose job involves fighting, who is on top of a fast brown animal? Alice then grabs the yellow pawn and places it on 'place/country' with a yellow cube on 'USA.' Bob shouts 'Cowboy!' Nailed it! Three sub-concepts working together made a complex word guessable.

walkthrough.title: Why That Worked
walkthrough.text: Let's break down why that was such a great clue. The green main concept told us: man, job, fighting. The blue sub-concept told us: big, brown, fast animal. The green 'above' cube connected the man to the animal. And the yellow sub-concept pinpointed the country. Each layer added exactly the information needed without overwhelming. That's the art of Concept.

walkthrough.title: The Game Continues
walkthrough.text: Teams keep rotating clockwise. Carol and Bob draw the next card. As the game goes on, you'll find that guessers get better at reading the icons and clue-givers get more creative with their combinations. Don't be afraid to try unusual icon associations -- as long as the guessers can follow your logic, anything goes!

walkthrough.title: Congratulations!
walkthrough.text: You've seen how to give clues with the main concept, how to layer sub-concepts for complex words, and three common mistakes to avoid: dumping too many clues at once, forgetting to use sub-concepts, and accidentally talking or gesturing. You're ready to play Concept for real! Remember, the points are fun but the real joy is in those 'aha!' moments when someone cracks your clue.

walkthrough.title: Before You Play
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive in, here are three things that can help.

First -- check out General Tips for clue-giving strategies and common icon tricks.

Second -- reference the Appendix for a quick guide to pawn types, card categories, and board icons.

And most importantly -- ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Play
bullets: Check out General Tips for clue-giving strategies
Reference the Appendix for icon and pawn quick-reference
Ask your personal GM Guide via Q&A tab for instant answers

walkthrough.title: Start Broad, Then Narrow
walkthrough.text: The most effective clue-giving strategy is to go from general to specific. Always place the main concept pawn on the broadest category first -- animal, person, place, object, food. Then add cubes one at a time to narrow it down. This gives guessers a logical path to follow instead of dumping a pile of random associations on them. Think of it like playing 20 Questions: start with the big categories and zero in from there.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Clues Before Placing
walkthrough.text: Before you touch any pieces, take a moment with your teammate to quietly discuss your approach. Which icons will you use? In what order? Will you need sub-concepts? Having a plan prevents the common trap of placing cubes randomly and confusing everyone. A quick 30-second huddle with your partner can make the difference between a smooth guess and a frustrating stalemate.

walkthrough.title: Use Sub-Concepts for Complex Words
walkthrough.text: If your word has multiple distinct aspects -- like a famous person (profession + nationality + era) or a specific place (type of place + country + feature) -- you almost certainly need sub-concepts. The main concept covers the primary category, and each sub-concept adds a separate thread. Don't try to cram everything into the green set. The game gives you four extra color sets for a reason!

walkthrough.title: Icons Have Multiple Meanings
walkthrough.text: This is where Concept gets really creative. The ice cube icon can mean 'cold,' but it can also mean 'winter,' 'frozen,' or even 'ice cream.' The player aids list some meanings, but you're not limited to those. Any association that your fellow players might understand is fair game. As your group plays more, you'll develop your own shared vocabulary of icon meanings. That's part of the fun!

walkthrough.title: Read the Room
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to what guessers are saying. If they're headed in the wrong direction, consider removing cubes and trying a different approach rather than piling on more clues. If they're close -- like guessing 'horse' when the answer is 'cowboy' -- place cubes that build on their correct instinct rather than contradicting it. Your team can say 'yes' when someone's getting warm, so use that strategically.

walkthrough.title: As a Guesser, Think in Categories
walkthrough.text: When you're guessing, start by identifying the broad category from the main concept pawn. Is it an animal? A person? A place? Then look at what the green cubes are telling you about that category. When sub-concepts appear, ask yourself how they relate to the main idea. Don't fixate on your first guess -- if it's wrong, step back and reconsider what the icons might mean in a different context.

walkthrough.title: Don't Overthink Easy Words
walkthrough.text: New teams sometimes make Easy words harder than they need to be by using too many icons or overly clever associations. If the word is 'milk,' you don't need four sub-concepts -- food + white + liquid gets there in seconds. Save the elaborate multi-concept setups for Hard and Challenging words. Simple words deserve simple clues.

walkthrough.title: Embrace the Chaos
walkthrough.text: Concept is a party game, and the best moments come from misunderstandings, wild guesses, and surprising icon associations. Don't get frustrated if your brilliant clue strategy falls flat -- laugh about it and try again. The editor's note in the rulebook says it best: the designers eventually abandoned scoring entirely and just enjoyed guessing. Points are just a framework -- the real game is the communication puzzle.

bullets: Place main concept pawn on the broadest category first
Add cubes one at a time to narrow down
Think of it like 20 Questions -- general to specific

bullets: Discuss strategy quietly with your teammate first

A 30-second huddle prevents confusion later

Main concept = primary category; sub-concepts = context
Don't cram everything into the green set -- use all colors

summary.title: Icons Have Multiple Meanings
bullets: Ice cube can mean cold, winter, frozen, or ice cream
Player aids are suggestions, not the complete list
Your group will develop shared icon meanings over time

summary.title: Read the Room
bullets: Listen to guesses -- adjust your approach based on responses
Remove and re-place cubes if guessers are going wrong
Build on correct instincts when guessers are close

summary.title: As a Guesser, Think in Categories
bullets: Identify the broad category from the main concept pawn first
Look at how green cubes narrow the main idea
Consider how sub-concepts relate to the main idea

summary.title: Don't Overthink Easy Words
bullets: Simple words deserve simple clues
Save elaborate multi-concept setups for Hard/Challenging
'Milk' only needs food + white + liquid, not four sub-concepts

summary.title: Embrace the Chaos
bullets: Best moments come from misunderstandings and wild guesses
Don't get frustrated when clues fall flat -- laugh and retry
Designers themselves dropped scoring and played for fun

walkthrough.title: Master the Order of Cube Placement
walkthrough.text: Advanced clue-givers know that the order in which cubes are placed carries meaning. The rulebook specifically calls this out. Place the most important distinguishing feature first, then add secondary details. If you're cluing 'polar bear,' place the main concept on 'animal,' then 'white' before 'large' -- the color is more distinctive than the size. Guessers naturally weight earlier clues more heavily, so front-load your most critical icons.

walkthrough.title: The Reset Strategy
walkthrough.text: Don't be afraid to wipe the board clean and start over mid-clue. The rulebook explicitly allows this. If guessers are stuck or heading in the completely wrong direction, a fresh approach can be more efficient than piling on more cubes. Think of it like erasing a whiteboard -- sometimes a clean slate communicates better than layers of corrections. Experienced teams use resets as a strategic tool, not a sign of failure.

walkthrough.title: Cluing Abstract Concepts
walkthrough.text: Hard and Challenging words often include abstract ideas like 'freedom,' 'love,' or 'democracy.' These require creative icon associations. Think about what concrete symbols, colors, or objects people associate with the abstract idea. 'Freedom' might use the bird icon (flying free), the USA icon (Statue of Liberty), or the 'open/wide' icon. Layer sub-concepts to build the abstract meaning from concrete pieces -- that's where the real artistry of Concept lives.

walkthrough.title: Cluing Famous People
walkthrough.text: The rulebook shows Vincent van Gogh as an example: man + arts + cut ear. For famous people, establish the category first (man/woman, era, profession), then use sub-concepts for distinguishing features. A musician might need sub-concepts for their instrument, nationality, and era. An actor might need their most famous movie. The key is finding the one or two traits that uniquely identify the person -- what makes them different from every other person in that category?

walkthrough.title: Cluing Multi-Word Phrases
walkthrough.text: Concept cards include phrases, not just single words. For multi-word concepts like 'red wine' or 'ice cream,' you often need to treat each word as its own concept thread. Use the main concept for the primary noun and sub-concepts for modifiers. 'Red wine' could be: green pawn on 'drink/liquid,' green cube on 'wine/alcohol,' then a blue sub-concept adding 'red.' The cleaner you separate the parts, the easier guessers can recombine them.

walkthrough.title: Strategic Use of the Third Player
walkthrough.text: The rulebook says that if guessing stalls, you can invite a third player to join the team. Use this strategically. The third player has been watching from the guesser side, so they know what associations are confusing people. They bring a guesser's perspective to the clue-giving, which often breaks the logjam. Don't wait too long to invite help -- if your first approach isn't working after a minute, a fresh mind can save the round.

walkthrough.title: Maximizing VP Efficiency
walkthrough.text: If you're playing with points, think about VP efficiency. As a clue-giver, you want your word to be guessed (1 VP each for you and your partner). As a guesser, you want to be first to shout the answer (2 VP). Pay attention to the clue-givers' style -- do they always start with the category? Do they favor certain icons? The more you learn your friends' patterns, the faster you'll guess their clues. Meanwhile, when clue-giving, choose words you're confident the group can guess rather than showing off with Challenging words nobody gets.

walkthrough.title: Advanced Icon Combinations
walkthrough.text: The board's icons can be combined in surprisingly powerful ways. The 'opposite/negation' icon lets you flip any meaning -- 'not big' means small, 'not old' means new. Spatial icons like 'inside,' 'above,' and 'between' can describe physical relationships. Time icons can distinguish past from present. Color icons can represent flags, brands, or emotions. The more you play, the more you'll discover icon combinations that your group understands intuitively. That shared language is what makes experienced Concept groups so much faster than new ones.

summary.title: Master the Order of Cube Placement
bullets: Place the most distinctive feature first
Guessers weight earlier clues more heavily
Front-load critical icons, add secondary details after

summary.title: The Reset Strategy
bullets: Wiping the board and starting over is explicitly allowed
A fresh approach beats piling on more confusing cubes
Experienced teams use resets strategically, not as failure

summary.title: Cluing Abstract Concepts
bullets: Abstract ideas need concrete symbol associations
'Freedom' might use bird (flying), USA (Statue of Liberty)
Layer sub-concepts to build abstract meaning from concrete pieces

summary.title: Cluing Famous People
bullets: Establish category first (gender, era, profession)
Use sub-concepts for distinguishing features
Find the 1-2 traits that uniquely identify the person

summary.title: Cluing Multi-Word Phrases
bullets: Treat each word as its own concept thread
Main concept for the primary noun, sub-concepts for modifiers
Cleanly separating parts helps guessers recombine them

summary.title: Strategic Use of the Third Player
bullets: Third player brings the guesser's perspective to clue-giving
They know what associations are confusing people
Don't wait too long -- invite help after about a minute

summary.title: Maximizing VP Efficiency
bullets: As guesser: learn friends' clue-giving patterns for speed
As team: choose words you're confident the group can guess
Confident Easy words beat failed Challenging words for points

summary.title: Advanced Icon Combinations
bullets: Negation icon flips any meaning (not big = small)
Spatial icons describe physical relationships (inside, above)
Color icons represent flags, brands, or emotions
Shared group language develops over multiple games

entries.definition: The large green question mark pawn. Always placed first on the board to indicate the broadest category of the word being clued. Only one main concept pawn exists per game.
10 green cubes used to add detail to the main concept. Placed on icons after the green question mark pawn to narrow down the main idea.
Four exclamation mark pawns in blue, red, yellow, and black. Each starts a new thread of clues that clarifies or adds context to the main concept.
8 cubes per sub-concept color (blue, red, yellow, black). Placed on icons to build out the sub-concept started by the matching exclamation mark pawn.
110 double-sided cards, each listing 9 words or phrases. Words are divided into three difficulty categories: Easy, Hard, and Challenging.
Victory point token worth 2 VPs. Awarded to the first player who correctly guesses a word. There are exactly 12 in the game; when all 12 are claimed, the game ends.
Victory point token worth 1 VP. Awarded to each member of the clue-giving team when their word is correctly guessed.
The central board covered with universal icons representing colors, sizes, categories, actions, locations, and more. All clue-giving happens by placing pieces on this board.
2 reference cards listing possible meanings for the universal icons on the game board. The listed meanings are suggestions, not an exhaustive list.
A bowl included in the game for storing all pawns and cubes between rounds. Pieces return here after each word is guessed or abandoned.
The simplest difficulty category on Concept cards (marked in green). Recommended for first games. Typically concrete nouns and common objects like 'milk,' 'dinosaur,' or 'guitar.'
The medium difficulty category on Concept cards (marked in orange). Includes less obvious words, multi-word phrases, and concepts requiring more creative icon use.
The highest difficulty category on Concept cards (marked in red). Requires mastery of the game by all players. Often includes abstract concepts, famous people, and complex phrases.
The primary clue thread, always started with the green question mark pawn. Represents the broadest description of the word being guessed. Green cubes add detail to the main concept.

An additional clue thread started by placing a colored exclamation mark pawn (blue, red, yellow, or black). Used to add context, specify location, describe associated objects, or narrow down the main concept.

Two neighboring players who work together to give clues each round. They secretly choose a word and place pieces on the board. Team members can consult quietly and either can place pieces at any time.
All non-team players shout guesses freely with no turn order. There is no limit on guesses and no penalty for wrong answers. Players can guess as often as they like.
The only verbal communication allowed from the clue-giving team. Team members can say 'yes' or nod when a guesser is on the right track. No other words, gestures, or hints are permitted.
The team can choose to remove all pawns and cubes from the board at any time and restart their clue-giving with a completely different approach to the same word.
If guessing stalls, the team can invite a third player to join and help give clues. This player adds a guesser's perspective to the clue-giving process.
If nobody can guess the word even with help, the team abandons it and no points are scored. The next team clockwise takes over.
The order in which cubes are placed on the board can convey meaning and emphasis. The rulebook encourages removing and re-placing cubes to change the order if needed.
Board icons representing colors (white, black, red, blue, green, yellow, etc.). Can indicate the literal color of an object, a flag, a brand association, or an emotional connotation.
Board icons representing size (large/big, small/little). Used to describe the physical size of the concept being clued.
Board icons for broad categories like animal, person/man/woman, place, food/drink, object/tool. These are typically where the main concept pawn is placed first.
Board icons representing time concepts (old/past, new/present, fast, slow). 'Old' can mean ancient, historical, or from long ago. Useful for cluing historical figures or vintage items.
Board icons for spatial relationships (above/on top, below/under, inside, outside, between). Used to describe physical arrangements or relationships between concepts.
Board icons for temperature (hot/warm, cold/cool). The cold/ice cube icon has multiple uses: literal cold, winter season, frozen items, or the concept of ice itself.
Board icons distinguishing fictional from real. Useful for cluing movie characters, book characters, mythological creatures, or distinguishing real people from fictional ones.
Board icons representing countries, flags, or geographic locations. Combined with color cubes, can indicate specific nations (e.g., blue + white + red for France).
The game ends immediately once all 12 Double VP tokens have been claimed. Players count their total VPs from both Double and Single tokens. Highest total wins.
The first player to correctly guess the word receives 1 Double VP token worth 2 victory points.
When a word is correctly guessed, each member of the clue-giving team receives 1 Single VP token worth 1 victory point.
If a word is abandoned (nobody guesses correctly), no victory points are awarded to anyone that round. The next team simply takes over.
A house rule suggested by the rulebook: award 1-2 additional VPs to the team and guesser for successfully guessing Hard or Challenging words.

The game designers note that during extensive testing, they abandoned scoring and played purely for the enjoyment of guessing. The rulebook explicitly encourages this approach if your group prefers it.
